question_html:

options.text: E on the LHS of the above equation will have a contribution from all charges while q on the RHS will have a contribution from q 2 and q 4 only.

If  {tex}\oint_{s} \mathbf{E} \cdot d \mathbf{s}=0{/tex} over a surface, then

  2. the electric field inside the surface is necessarily uniform.

solution_text: From Gauss' law, {tex}\int_{S} E \cdot d s=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{o}}{/tex} .....equation (i) Where q is charge enclosed by the surface. {tex}\int_{s} E d s=0{/tex} (given) by comparing the given equation with equation (i) we get, q = 0 i.e. , net charge enclosed by the surface must be zero thus total electric flux through a closed surface is zero Hence, all other charges must necessarily be outside the surface.

by comparing the given equation with equation (i)

question_html:

  1. {tex}\oint_{\mathrm{s}} \mathbf{E} \cdot \mathrm{d} \mathbf{s} \neq 0{/tex} on any surface.
  2. {tex}\oint_{s} \mathbf{E} \cdot d \mathbf{s}=0{/tex} if the charge is outside the surface.
  3. {tex}\oint_{s} \mathbf{E} \cdot d \mathbf{s}{/tex} could not be defined.
  4. {tex}\oint_{\mathrm{s}} \mathbf{E} \cdot \mathrm{d} \mathbf{s}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} if charges of magnitude q were inside the surface.
solution_text: According to the Gauss' law, we know that {tex}\int_{S} E \cdot d s=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{o}}{/tex} Where q is the charge enclosed by the surface. If the charge is outside the surface, then the net charge enclosed by the surface is q = 0, therefore, {tex}\int_{s} E d s=0{/tex} then q=0, i.e., net charge enclosed by the surface must be zero.Hence option (b) and (d) are correct. Electric flux doesn't depend on the nature or type of charge.

Where q is the charge enclosed by the surface.

question_text: Consider a region inside which there are various types of charges but the total charge is zero. At points outside the region: the electric field is necessarily zero. the electric field is due to the dipole moment of the charge distribution only. the dominant electric field {tex}\propto \frac{1}{r^{3}}{/tex} for large r, where r is the distance from an origin in this region. the work done to move a charged particle along a closed path, away from the region, will be zero.
question_html:
Consider a region inside which there are various types of charges but the total charge is zero. At points outside the region:

  1. the electric field is necessarily zero.
  2. the electric field is due to the dipole moment of the charge distribution only.
  3. the dominant electric field  {tex}\propto \frac{1}{r^{3}}{/tex} for large r, where r is the distance from an origin in this region.
  4. the work done to move a charged particle along a closed path, away from the region, will be zero.
solution_text: From Gauss law, we know that, ∮E.ds = q/∈ 0 The above equation represents the electric field is due to all the charges present both inside as well as outside the surface. Hence if q (enclosed) =0, it can not be said that the electric field is necessarily zero. Here, the net charge is zero in the region. We can assume that it contains one or more than one electric dipole. So, at points outside the region, the dominant electric field is {tex}\propto \frac{1}{r^{3}}{/tex} for large r. Also, the electric field is a conservative field, work done to move a charged particle along a closed path, away from the region will be zero. Hence, statement C and D are correct.

question_text: A positive charge Q is uniformly distributed along a circular ring of radius R. A small test charge q is placed at the centre of the ring (Fig.). Then If q > 0 and is displaced away from the centre in the plane of the ring, it will be pushed back towards the centre. If q < 0 and is displaced away from the centre in the plane of the ring, it will never return to the centre and will continue moving till it hits the ring. If q < 0, it will perform SHM for small displacement along the axis. q at the centre of the ring is in an unstable equilibrium within the plane of the ring for q > 0.
question_html:
A positive charge Q is uniformly distributed along a circular ring of radius R. A small test charge q is placed at the centre of the ring (Fig.). Then

  1. If q > 0 and is displaced away from the centre in the plane of the ring, it will be pushed back towards the centre.
  2. If q < 0 and is displaced away from the centre in the plane of the ring, it will never return to the centre and will continue moving till it hits the ring.
  3. If q < 0, it will perform SHM for small displacement along the axis.
  4. q at the centre of the ring is in an unstable equilibrium within the plane of the ring for q > 0.
solution_text: Here, the positive charge Q is uniformly distributed on the outer surface of the ring. Thus, the electric field inside the ring is zero. So, the effect of the electric force on charge q due to the positive charge Q is at the centre zero. When q < 0, the force between Q and q is attractive. If q is displaced away from the centre in the plane of the ring, the net force on one side decrease and other side increase by the same amount. So, it will be pushed back towards the centre. When this charge is displaced towards the axis of the ring, it will gain potential energy and will be pulled by the ring (towards its centre). Due to the increase in kinetic energy, it will cross the centre and again gains potential energy. Afterwards, it will be again pulled towards the centre and this process (SHM) will continue due to interconversion of energies. When q > 0, the force between Q and q is repulsive. If q is displaced away from the centre in the plane of the ring, again, the net force on one side decrease and other side increase by the same amount. So, it will be pushed back towards the centre. Within the plane of the ring, the equilibrium is stable as the charge q tends to move back to its position. Hence, options a, b, and c are correct.
solution_html:
Here, the positive charge Q is uniformly distributed on the outer surface of the ring. Thus, the electric field inside the ring is zero. So, the effect of the electric force on charge q due to the positive charge Q is at the centre zero.
When q < 0, the force between Q and q is attractive. If q is displaced away from the centre in the plane of the ring, the net force on one side decrease and other side increase by the same amount. So, it will be pushed back towards the centre.
When this charge is displaced towards the axis of the ring, it will gain potential energy and will be pulled by the ring (towards its centre). Due to the increase in kinetic energy, it will cross the centre and again gains potential energy. Afterwards, it will be again pulled towards the centre and this process (SHM) will continue due to interconversion of energies.
When q > 0, the force between Q and q is repulsive. If q is displaced away from the centre in the plane of the ring, again, the net force on one side decrease and other side increase by the same amount. So, it will be pushed back towards the centre.
Within the plane of the ring, the equilibrium is stable as the charge q tends to move back to its position.
Hence, options a, b, and c are correct.

question_text: A metallic spherical shell has an inner radius R 1 and outer radius R 2 . A charge Q is placed at the centre of the shell. What will be the surface charge density on the (i) inner surface, and (ii) outer surface of the shell?
question_html: A metallic spherical shell has an inner radius R1 and outer radius R2. A charge Q is placed at the centre of the shell. What will be the surface charge density on the (i) inner surface, and (ii) outer surface of the shell?
solution_text: Due to induction by charge +Q at centre, the inner surface acquires -Q charge and outer surface of shell will acquire +Q charge. So, Surface charge density on outside surface, {tex}\sigma{/tex} = {tex}\frac{+Q}{4 \pi R_{2}^{2}}{/tex} Surface charge density on inside surface, {tex}\sigma{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-Q}{4 \pi R_{1}^{2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Due to induction by charge +Q at centre, the inner surface acquires -Q charge and outer surface of shell will acquire +Q charge. So,

  1. Surface charge density on outside surface, {tex}\sigma{/tex} = {tex}\frac{+Q}{4 \pi R_{2}^{2}}{/tex}
  2. Surface charge density on inside surface, {tex}\sigma{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-Q}{4 \pi R_{1}^{2}}{/tex}

question_text: If the total charge enclosed by a surface is zero, does it imply that the electric field everywhere on the surface is zero? Conversely, if the electric field everywhere on a surface is zero, does it imply that net charge inside is zero?
question_html: If the total charge enclosed by a surface is zero, does it imply that the electric field everywhere on the surface is zero? Conversely, if the electric field everywhere on a surface is zero, does it imply that net charge inside is zero?
solution_text: By Gauss's law of electrostatics, {tex}\oint_{s} E d s=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Consider a Gaussian surface enclosing charges q 1 , q 2 , q 3 and q 1 + q 2 + q 3 = 0. But electric field on R.H.S due to q4 will not be zero. But, on L.H.S., it will be zero. So if the total charge enclosed by a surface is zero, the electric field everywhere on the surface is not zero. If electric field on Gaussian surface is zero, then, in above case, it is possible only when q 4 = 0, i.e., if everywhere, on Gaussian surface, electric field is zero then net charge will be zero.
solution_html:
By Gauss's law of electrostatics,
{tex}\oint_{s} E d s=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}

Consider a Gaussian surface enclosing charges q1, q2, q3 and q1 + q2 + q3 = 0. But electric field on R.H.S due to q4 will not be zero. But, on L.H.S., it will be zero. So if the total charge enclosed by a surface is zero, the electric field everywhere on the surface is not zero.
If electric field on Gaussian surface is zero, then, in above case, it is possible only when q4 = 0, i.e., if everywhere, on Gaussian surface, electric field is zero then net charge will be zero.

  3. When the charge is placed at point C of the cube then only 1/2th portion of the charge lies inside the Gaussian surface. So, total flux through the faces of the given cube = {tex}\frac q {2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}.
  4. When the charge is placed at point D of the cube then only 1/2th portion of the charge lies inside the Gaussian surface. So, total flux through the faces of the given cube = {tex}\frac q {2 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}.

question_text: A paisa coin is made up of Al-Mg alloy and weighs 0.75g. It has a square shape and its diagonal measures 17 mm. It is electrically neutral and contains equal amounts of positive and negative charges. Treating the paisa coins made up of only Al, find the magnitude of an equal number of positive and negative charges. What conclusion do you draw from this magnitude?
question_html: A paisa coin is made up of Al-Mg alloy and weighs 0.75g. It has a square shape and its diagonal measures 17 mm. It is electrically neutral and contains equal amounts of positive and negative charges. Treating the paisa coins made up of only Al, find the magnitude of an equal number of positive and negative charges. What conclusion do you draw from this magnitude?
solution_text: A 0.75 g paisa coin now is made with Al only. So, the mass of a paisa coin = 0.75g Atomic mass of Al {tex}\cong{/tex} 27 a.m.u So, number of moles in 0.75 g = {tex}\frac{0.75}{27}{/tex} mole Number of Al atoms in coin = N = {tex}\frac{0.75}{27} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}{/tex} atoms Atomic number of Al = 13 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of electrons (negative charge) and protons (positive charge) = 13. So, number of either proton or electron in a coin = {tex}\frac{13 \times 0.75}{27} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}{/tex} Magnitude of charge on a proton or electron = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 C So, total charge (either positive or negative) = {tex}\frac{13 \times 75 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}}{{27} \times 100}{/tex} = 3.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 C Either positive or negative charge on a coin = 3.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 C It concludes that even a 0.75 g Al contains an enormous amount of positive and negative charges and equal in magnitude.
solution_html:
A 0.75 g paisa coin now is made with Al only.
So, the mass of a paisa coin = 0.75g
Atomic mass of Al {tex}\cong{/tex} 27 a.m.u
So, number of moles in 0.75 g = {tex}\frac{0.75}{27}{/tex} mole
Number of Al atoms in coin = N
= {tex}\frac{0.75}{27} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}{/tex} atoms
Atomic number of Al = 13
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of electrons (negative charge) and protons (positive charge) = 13.
So, number of either proton or electron in a coin = {tex}\frac{13 \times 0.75}{27} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}{/tex}
Magnitude of charge on a proton or electron = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C
So, total charge (either positive or negative)
= {tex}\frac{13 \times 75 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}}{{27} \times 100}{/tex}
= 3.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 C
Either positive or negative charge on a coin = 3.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 C
It concludes that even a 0.75 g Al contains an enormous amount of positive and negative charges and equal in magnitude.

question_text: Figure shows a crystal unit of cesium chloride, CsCl. The Cs atoms are situated at the corners of a cube of side 0.4 nm, whereas a Cl atom is situated at the center of the cube. The Cs atoms are deficient in one electron while the Cl atom carries an excess electron. What is the net electric field (force) on the Cl atom due to eight Cs atoms? Suppose that the Cs atom at the corner A is missing. What is the net force on the Cl atom due to seven remaining Cs atoms?
question_html:
Figure shows a crystal unit of cesium chloride, CsCl. The Cs atoms are situated at the corners of a cube of side 0.4 nm, whereas a Cl atom is situated at the center of the cube. The Cs atoms are deficient in one electron while the Cl atom carries an excess electron.

  1. What is the net electric field (force) on the Cl atom due to eight Cs atoms?
  2. Suppose that the Cs atom at the corner A is missing. What is the net force on the Cl atom due to seven remaining Cs atoms?
solution_text: Zero, from symmetry. As Cs ions are located symmetrically around the Cl ion, the net force on the Cl ion will be zero. When all the Cs ions are in their positions, the net force on the Cl ion is zero. It follows that net force on the Cl ion due to the Cs ions at the seven corners must be equal and opposite to the force exerted due to the Cs ion at the eighth corner alone. In other words, when the positively charged Cs ion at the corner A is missing, the net force on the Cl ion will be equal to that due to a negatively charged Cs ion placed at the corner A. If r is the distance of the comer A from the Cl ion, then r = {tex}\sqrt{{(0.2)}^2+{(0.2)}^2+{(0.2)}^2}=0.346\;nm{/tex} Hence, the net force on the Cl ion due to the seven remaining Cs ions is given by, F net = {tex}q_1q_2\over 4\pi\epsilon_0r^2{/tex} F net = {tex}9\times10^9\times\frac{{(1.6\times10^{-19})}^2}{{(0.346\times10^{-9})}^2}=1.92\times10^{-9}\;N{/tex} The force will be repulsive in nature i.e. directed from A to Cl ion.
solution_html:
1. Zero, from symmetry. As Cs ions are located symmetrically around the Cl ion, the net force on the Cl ion will be zero.
  2. When all the Cs ions are in their positions, the net force on the Cl ion is zero. It follows that net force on the Cl ion due to the Cs ions at the seven corners must be equal and opposite to the force exerted due to the Cs ion at the eighth corner alone. In other words, when the positively charged Cs ion at the corner A is missing, the net force on the Cl ion will be equal to that due to a negatively charged Cs ion placed at the corner A.
    If r is the distance of the comer A from the Cl ion, then
    r = {tex}\sqrt{{(0.2)}^2+{(0.2)}^2+{(0.2)}^2}=0.346\;nm{/tex}
    Hence, the net force on the Cl ion due to the seven remaining Cs ions is given by, Fnet = {tex}q_1q_2\over 4\pi\epsilon_0r^2{/tex}
    Fnet = {tex}9\times10^9\times\frac{{(1.6\times10^{-19})}^2}{{(0.346\times10^{-9})}^2}=1.92\times10^{-9}\;N{/tex}
    The force will be repulsive in nature i.e. directed from A to Cl ion.

question_text: Two charges q and -3q are placed fixed on x-axis separated by distance d . Where should a third charge 2q be placed such that it will not experience any force?
question_html: Two charges q and -3q are placed fixed on x-axis separated by distance d. Where should a third charge 2q be placed such that it will not experience any force?
solution_text: If we place the third charge 2q between A and B the direction of the force on 2q due to A and B on C will be same. So the net force cannot be zero, so the charge q cannot be placed between A and B. If 2q is placed the right side of A then r AC > r BC as q A < q B . So F CA < F CB always as the direction of F CA is towards right and F CB is left so, F CA + F CB {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 we can not obtain required condition. Now consider 2q at the C to left of q at distance x from q. Force on 2q at C (left of q) is in opposite direction so net force will be zero if magnitude is equal. So, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} F CA + F CB = 0 or F CA = - F CB {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{K q_{C} q_{A}}{r^{2} C A}=\frac{-K q_{C} q_{B}}{r^{2} C B}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 q q}{x^{2}}=\frac{-2 q(-3 q)}{(x+d)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 q^{2}}{x^{2}}=\frac{6 q^{2}}{(x+d)^{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x^{2}}=\frac{3}{(x+d)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x 2 = x 2 + d 2 + 2xd {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x 2 - 2xd - d 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{+2 d \pm \sqrt{(-2 d)^{2}-4.2 \cdot\left(-d^{2}\right)}}{2.2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{+2 d \pm \sqrt{4 d^{2}+8 d^{2}}}{4}=\frac{2 d \pm 2 d \sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{d(1 \pm \sqrt{3})}{2}{/tex} So, {tex}x=\frac{d}{2}(1+\sqrt{3}){/tex} m to the left of q, it will not experience any force
solution_html:
1. If we place the third charge 2q between A and B the direction of the force on 2q due to A and B on C will be same.
    So the net force cannot be zero, so the charge q cannot be placed between A and B.
  2. If 2q is placed the right side of A then rAC > rBC as qA < qB. So FCA < FCB  always as the direction of FCA is towards right and FCB is left so, FCA + FCB  {tex}\ne{/tex} 0 we can not obtain required condition.
  3. Now consider 2q at the C to left of q at distance x from q.
    Force on 2q at C (left of q) is in opposite direction so net force will be zero if magnitude is equal. So,
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} FCA + FCB = 0 or FCA = - FCB 
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{K q_{C} q_{A}}{r^{2} C A}=\frac{-K q_{C} q_{B}}{r^{2} C B}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 q q}{x^{2}}=\frac{-2 q(-3 q)}{(x+d)^{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 q^{2}}{x^{2}}=\frac{6 q^{2}}{(x+d)^{2}} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x^{2}}=\frac{3}{(x+d)^{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x2 = x2 + d2 + 2xd
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x2 - 2xd - d2 = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{+2 d \pm \sqrt{(-2 d)^{2}-4.2 \cdot\left(-d^{2}\right)}}{2.2}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{+2 d \pm \sqrt{4 d^{2}+8 d^{2}}}{4}=\frac{2 d \pm 2 d \sqrt{3}}{4}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x=\frac{d(1 \pm \sqrt{3})}{2}{/tex}
    So,  {tex}x=\frac{d}{2}(1+\sqrt{3}){/tex} m to the left of q, it will not experience any force

question_text: Figure shows the electric field lines around three point charges A, B and C. Which charges are positive? Which charge has the largest magnitude? Why? In which region or regions of the picture could the electric field be zero? (i) near A, (ii) near B, (iii) near C, (iv) nowhere. Justify your answer.
question_html:
Figure shows the electric field lines around three point charges A, B and C.

  1. Which charges are positive?
  2. Which charge has the largest magnitude? Why?
  3. In which region or regions of the picture could the electric field be zero? (i) near A, (ii) near B, (iii) near C, (iv) nowhere. Justify your answer.
solution_text: Since the electric field lines emanating from A and C, therefore the charges A and C are positive. From a larger charge, relatively greater number of electric field lines emanate from charge C. Therefore, the charge C has the largest magnitude. The electric field cannot be zero between the unlike charges pairs, such as A and B or B and C. It can be zero between two like charges (i.e. A and C) only. Further, the point, where the electric field is zero, would be closer to smaller charge and farther away from the larger charge. Therefore, the electric field is zero near the charge A.
solution_html:
1. Since the electric field lines emanating from A and C, therefore the charges A and C are positive.
  2. From a larger charge, relatively greater number of electric field lines emanate from charge C. Therefore, the charge C has the largest magnitude.
  3. The electric field cannot be zero between the unlike charges pairs, such as A and B or B and C. It can be zero between two like charges (i.e. A and C) only. Further, the point, where the electric field is zero, would be closer to smaller charge and farther away from the larger charge.
    Therefore, the electric field is zero near the charge A.

question_text: Five charges, q each are placed at the corners of a regular pentagon of side a (Fig.). What will be the electric field at O, the centre of the pentagon? What will be the electric field at O if the charge from one of the corners (say A) is removed? What will be the electric field at O if the charge q at A is replaced by –q? How would your answer to (a) be affected if pentagon is replaced by n- sided regular polygon with charge q at each of its corners?
question_html:
Five charges, q each are placed at the corners of a regular pentagon of side a (Fig.).

  1.  
    1. What will be the electric field at O, the centre of the pentagon?
    2. What will be the electric field at O if the charge from one of the corners (say A) is removed?
    3. What will be the electric field at O if the charge q at A is replaced by –q?
  2. How would your answer to (a) be affected if pentagon is replaced by n- sided regular polygon with charge q at each of its corners?
solution_text: Here, point O is equidistant from all the charges at the endpoint of the pentagon. Thus, due to symmetry, the forces due to all the charges are canceled out; As a result, the electric field at O is zero.When charge q is removed, it is equivalent to placing a -q charge at the same point. The electric field at point O would be {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{q}{4 \pi s_{0} r^{2}} {/tex} along {tex}\overrightarrow{O A}{/tex} If charge q at A is replaced by -q, which is equivalent to placing two negative charges i.e., -2q at the same point. So the value of the electric field would be {tex}\overrightarrow{E^{\prime}}=\frac{2 q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{o} r^{2}} \text { along } \overrightarrow{O A}{/tex} If pentagon shown here is replaced by any n sided regular polygon with charge q at each of its corners, again charges are symmetrical about the centre thus the electric field at O would continue to be zero.
solution_html:
1. Here, point O is equidistant from all the charges at the endpoint of the pentagon. Thus, due to symmetry, the forces due to all the charges are canceled out; As a result, the electric field at O is zero.When charge q is removed, it is equivalent to placing a -q charge at the same point. The electric field at point O would be {tex}\vec{E}=\frac{q}{4 \pi s_{0} r^{2}} {/tex} along {tex}\overrightarrow{O A}{/tex}If charge q at A is replaced by -q, which is equivalent to placing two negative charges i.e., -2q at the same point. So the value of the electric field would be {tex}\overrightarrow{E^{\prime}}=\frac{2 q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{o} r^{2}} \text { along } \overrightarrow{O A}{/tex}
  2. If pentagon shown here is replaced by any n sided regular polygon with charge q at each of its corners, again charges are symmetrical about the centre thus the electric field at O would continue to be zero.

question_text: In 1959 Lyttleton and Bondi suggested that the expansion of the Universe could be explained if matter carried a net charge. Suppose that the Universe is made up of hydrogen atoms with a number density N, which is maintained a constant. Let the charge on the proton be: e p = – (1 + y)e where e is the electronic charge. Find the critical value of y such that expansion may start. Show that the velocity of expansion is proportional to the distance from the centre.
question_html:
In 1959 Lyttleton and Bondi suggested that the expansion of the Universe could be explained if matter carried a net charge. Suppose that the Universe is made up of hydrogen atoms with a number density N, which is maintained a constant. Let the charge on the proton be: ep = – (1 + y)e where e is the electronic charge.

  1. Find the critical value of y such that expansion may start.
  2. Show that the velocity of expansion is proportional to the distance from the centre.
solution_text: Suppose universe is a perfect sphere of radius R and its constituents are Hydrogen atom are distributed uniformly. As hydrogen atom contains one proton and one electron, charge on each hydrogen atom e H = e P + e = -(1 + y)e + e = -ye = |ye| According to Gauss' theorem. {tex}\oint E \cdot d s=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow E\left(4 \pi R^{2}\right)=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}}\left[\frac{4}{3}\left(\pi R^{3} N|y e|\right)\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow E=\frac{1}{3} \frac{N|y e| R}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} ...(i) Let, mass of each hydrogen atom {tex}\sim{/tex} m p= ( mass of a proton ), G R = gravitational field at distance R on the sphere. Then {tex}4 \pi R^{2} G_{R}=4 \pi G m_{p}\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}\right) N{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow G_{R}=\frac{-4}{3} \pi G m_{p} N R{/tex} ...(ii) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Gravitational force on this atom is {tex}F_{\sigma}=G_{R} \times m_{p}=\frac{-4}{3} \pi G m^{2} p N R{/tex} ...(iii) Coulomb force on hydrogen atom at R is {tex}F_{\mathrm{C}}=E(\mathrm{ye})=\frac{1}{3} \frac{N y^{2} e^{2} R}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} [from Eq.(i)] Expansion of the universe will start when coulomb repulsion F C > F G on the hydrogen atom. Now, the critical value of y (say y 2 to start expansion would be when, F C = F O {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{3} \frac{N y^{2} e^{2} R}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{4 \pi}{3} G m^{2}_{p} N R{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y^{2}=\left(4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\right) G\left(\frac{m_{p}}{e}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{9 \times 10^{9}} \times\left(6.67 \times 10^{-11}\right)\left(\frac{\left(1.66 \times 10^{-27}\right)^{2}}{\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{2}}\right)=79.8 \times 10^{-38}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\sqrt{79.8 \times 10^{-39}}=8.910^{-19}{/tex} y=10 -18 Hence 10 -18 is the required critical value of y corresponding to which expansion of universe would start. Net force experience by the hydrogen atom is given by: {tex}F_{N r}=F_{e}-F_{C}=\frac{1}{3} \frac{N y^{2} e^{2} R}{\varepsilon_{0}}-\frac{4 \pi}{3} G N m_{p}^{2} R{/tex} Due to net force, the hydrogen atom experiences an acceleration given by {tex}m_{p} \frac{d^{2} R}{d t^{2}}{/tex} {tex}F=m_{p} \frac{d^{2} R}{d t^{2}}=\frac{1}{3} \frac{N Y^{2} e^{2}}{\varepsilon_{0}} R-\frac{4 \pi}{3} G m_{p}^{2} N R{/tex} {tex}m_{p} \frac{d^{2} R}{d t^{2}}=\left(\frac{1}{3} \frac{N Y^{2} e^{2} R}{\varepsilon_{0}}-\frac{4 \pi}{3} G m_{p}^{2} N\right) R{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^{2} R}{d t^{2}}=\frac{1}{m_{p}}\left[\frac{1}{3} \frac{N Y^{2} e^{2} R}{\varepsilon_{0}}-\frac{4 \pi}{3} G m_{p}^{2} N\right] R=\alpha^{2} R{/tex} ...(iv) Where, {tex}\alpha^{2}=\frac{1}{m_{p}}\left[\frac{1}{3} \frac{N Y^{2} e^{2} R}{\varepsilon_{0}}-\frac{4 \pi}{3} G m_{p}^{2} N\right]{/tex} The general solution of Eq (iv) is given by {tex}R=A e^{\alpha t}+B^{-a t}{/tex} Here, we are looking for expansion, here, so B = 0 and {tex}R=A e^{\alpha t}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Velocity of expansion, {tex}v=\frac{d R}{d t}=A e^{\alpha t}(\alpha)=\alpha A e^{a t}=\alpha R{/tex} Hence, v {tex}\propto{/tex} R i.e., the velocity of expansion is proportional to the distance from the centre.
solution_html:
1. Suppose universe is a perfect sphere of radius R and its constituents are Hydrogen atom are distributed uniformly.

    As hydrogen atom contains one proton and one electron, charge on each hydrogen atom
    eH = eP + e = -(1 + y)e + e = -ye = |ye|
    According to Gauss' theorem.{tex}\oint E \cdot d s=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}

    Let, mass of each hydrogen atom {tex}\sim{/tex} mp=( mass of a proton ), GR= gravitational field at distance R on the sphere.
    Then {tex}4 \pi R^{2} G_{R}=4 \pi G m_{p}\left(\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3}\right) N{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow G_{R}=\frac{-4}{3} \pi G m_{p} N R{/tex} ...(ii)
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Gravitational force on this atom is {tex}F_{\sigma}=G_{R} \times m_{p}=\frac{-4}{3} \pi G m^{2} p N R{/tex} ...(iii)
    Coulomb force on hydrogen atom at R is {tex}F_{\mathrm{C}}=E(\mathrm{ye})=\frac{1}{3} \frac{N y^{2} e^{2} R}{\varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} [from Eq.(i)]
    Expansion of the universe will start when coulomb repulsion FC > FG on the hydrogen atom.
    Now, the critical value of y (say y2 to start expansion would be when, FC = FO
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{3} \frac{N y^{2} e^{2} R}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{4 \pi}{3} G m^{2}_{p} N R{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow y^{2}=\left(4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}\right) G\left(\frac{m_{p}}{e}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{9 \times 10^{9}} \times\left(6.67 \times 10^{-11}\right)\left(\frac{\left(1.66 \times 10^{-27}\right)^{2}}{\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)^{2}}\right)=79.8 \times 10^{-38}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow y=\sqrt{79.8 \times 10^{-39}}=8.910^{-19}{/tex}

    Hence  10-18  is the required critical value of y corresponding to which expansion of universe would start.

  2. Net force experience by the hydrogen atom is given by:

    {tex}F_{N r}=F_{e}-F_{C}=\frac{1}{3} \frac{N y^{2} e^{2} R}{\varepsilon_{0}}-\frac{4 \pi}{3} G N m_{p}^{2} R{/tex}
    Due to net force, the hydrogen atom experiences an acceleration given by {tex}m_{p} \frac{d^{2} R}{d t^{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}F=m_{p} \frac{d^{2} R}{d t^{2}}=\frac{1}{3} \frac{N Y^{2} e^{2}}{\varepsilon_{0}} R-\frac{4 \pi}{3} G m_{p}^{2} N R{/tex}
    {tex}m_{p} \frac{d^{2} R}{d t^{2}}=\left(\frac{1}{3} \frac{N Y^{2} e^{2} R}{\varepsilon_{0}}-\frac{4 \pi}{3} G m_{p}^{2} N\right) R{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{d^{2} R}{d t^{2}}=\frac{1}{m_{p}}\left[\frac{1}{3} \frac{N Y^{2} e^{2} R}{\varepsilon_{0}}-\frac{4 \pi}{3} G m_{p}^{2} N\right] R=\alpha^{2} R{/tex} ...(iv)
    Where, {tex}\alpha^{2}=\frac{1}{m_{p}}\left[\frac{1}{3} \frac{N Y^{2} e^{2} R}{\varepsilon_{0}}-\frac{4 \pi}{3} G m_{p}^{2} N\right]{/tex}
    The general solution of Eq (iv) is given by {tex}R=A e^{\alpha t}+B^{-a t}{/tex}
    Here, we are looking for expansion, here, so B = 0 and {tex}R=A e^{\alpha t}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Velocity of expansion,{tex}v=\frac{d R}{d t}=A e^{\alpha t}(\alpha)=\alpha A e^{a t}=\alpha R{/tex}
    Hence, v {tex}\propto{/tex} R i.e., the velocity of expansion is proportional to the distance from the centre.

question_text: Consider a sphere of radius R with charge density distributed as {tex}\rho{/tex} (r) = kr for r {tex}\leq{/tex} R = 0 for r > R Find the electric field at all points r. Suppose the total charge on the sphere is 2e where e is the electron charge. Where can two protons be embedded such that the force on each of them is zero? Assume that the introduction of the proton does not alter the negative charge distribution?
question_html:
Consider a sphere of radius R with charge density distributed as
{tex}\rho{/tex}(r) = kr for r {tex}\leq{/tex} R
= 0 for r > R

  1. Find the electric field at all points r.
  2. Suppose the total charge on the sphere is 2e where e is the electron charge. Where can two protons be embedded such that the force on each of them is zero? Assume that the introduction of the proton does not alter the negative charge distribution?
solution_text: The symmetry of the problem suggests that the electric field is radial. For points r < R, consider spherical Gaussian surfaces. Then on the surface Consider Gaussian surfaces as shown in the figure given below.let us consider a sphere of radius R. For points at r < R {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dS}}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}} \int p d V{/tex} Now, {tex}V=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \Rightarrow d V=3 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} d r \text { or } d V=4 \pi r^{2} d r{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \oint \mathrm{E} \cdot \mathrm{d} \mathrm{S}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}} 4 \pi K \int_{0}^{r} r^{3} d r[\because p(r)=K r]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow(E) 4 \pi r^{2}=\frac{4 \pi K}{\varepsilon_{0}} \frac{r^{4}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow E=\frac{1}{4 \varepsilon_{0}} K r^{2}{/tex} Here, charge density is positive. Therefore, the direction of E is radially outwards. For points r>R, electric field intensity is given by: {tex}\oint \mathrm{E} \cdot \mathrm{d} \mathrm{S}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}} \int p d V{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow E\left(4 \pi r^{2}\right)=\frac{4 \pi K}{\varepsilon_{0}} \int_{0}^{R} r^{3} d r=\frac{4 \pi K}{\varepsilon_{0}} \frac{R^{4}}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow E=\frac{K}{4 \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{R^{4}}{r^{2}}{/tex} Now charge density is again positive. So, the direction of E is radially outward. The two protons must be on the opposite sides of the center along a diameter. Suppose the protons are at a distance r from the center. {tex}q=\int_{0}^{R} p d V=\int_{0}^{R}(K r) 4 \pi r^{2} d r{/tex} {tex}q=4 \pi K \frac{R^{4}}{4}=2 e{/tex} {tex}\therefore k=\frac{2 e}{\pi R^{4}}{/tex} two protons are embedded at a distance r from the center of sphere. Consider the forces on proton 1. The attractive force due to the charge distribution is {tex}F_{1}=-e E=\frac{-e h r^{2}}{4 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex} Repulsive force on proton 1 due to proton 2 is {tex}F_{2}=\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}(2 r)^{2}}{/tex} Net force on proton 1, F Net =F 1 +F 2 {tex}F=\frac{-e k r^{2}}{4 \varepsilon_{0}}+\frac{e^{2}}{16 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}}{/tex} So, {tex}F_{\log }=\left[\frac{-e r^{2}}{4 \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Z e}{\pi R^{4}}+\frac{e^{2}}{16 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{4}}\right]{/tex} Thus, the net force on proton 1 will be zero, when {tex}\frac{e r^{2} 2^{*}}{4 \varepsilon_{0} \pi R^{4}}=\frac{e^{2}}{16 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r^{4}=\frac{R^{4}}{8}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r=\frac{R}{(8)^{1 / 4}}{/tex} Therefore, the distance of both the protons from the centre must be {tex}r=\frac{R}{(8)^{1 / 4}}{/tex} .
solution_html:
1. The symmetry of the problem suggests that the electric field is radial.
    For points r < R, consider spherical Gaussian surfaces. Then on the surface
    Consider Gaussian surfaces as shown in the figure given below.let us consider a sphere of radius R. 

    For points at r < R
    {tex}\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{dS}}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}} \int p d V{/tex}
    Now, {tex}V=\frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} \Rightarrow d V=3 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} d r \text { or } d V=4 \pi r^{2} d r{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \oint \mathrm{E} \cdot \mathrm{d} \mathrm{S}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}} 4 \pi K \int_{0}^{r} r^{3} d r[\because p(r)=K r]{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow(E) 4 \pi r^{2}=\frac{4 \pi K}{\varepsilon_{0}} \frac{r^{4}}{4}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow E=\frac{1}{4 \varepsilon_{0}} K r^{2}{/tex}
    Here, charge density is positive. Therefore, the direction of E is radially outwards.
    For points r>R, electric field intensity is given by:
    {tex}\oint \mathrm{E} \cdot \mathrm{d} \mathrm{S}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}} \int p d V{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow E\left(4 \pi r^{2}\right)=\frac{4 \pi K}{\varepsilon_{0}} \int_{0}^{R} r^{3} d r=\frac{4 \pi K}{\varepsilon_{0}} \frac{R^{4}}{4}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow E=\frac{K}{4 \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{R^{4}}{r^{2}}{/tex}
    Now charge density is again positive. So, the direction of E is radially outward.
  2. The two protons must be on the opposite sides of the center along a diameter. Suppose the protons are at a distance r from the center.

    {tex}q=\int_{0}^{R} p d V=\int_{0}^{R}(K r) 4 \pi r^{2} d r{/tex}
    {tex}q=4 \pi K \frac{R^{4}}{4}=2 e{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore k=\frac{2 e}{\pi R^{4}}{/tex}
     two protons are embedded at a distance r from the center of sphere. Consider the forces on proton 1. The attractive force due to the charge distribution is
    {tex}F_{1}=-e E=\frac{-e h r^{2}}{4 \varepsilon_{0}}{/tex}
    Repulsive force on proton 1 due to proton 2 is
    {tex}F_{2}=\frac{e^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}(2 r)^{2}}{/tex}
    Net force on proton 1, FNet=F1+F2
    {tex}F=\frac{-e k r^{2}}{4 \varepsilon_{0}}+\frac{e^{2}}{16 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}}{/tex}
    So, {tex}F_{\log }=\left[\frac{-e r^{2}}{4 \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{Z e}{\pi R^{4}}+\frac{e^{2}}{16 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{4}}\right]{/tex}
    Thus, the net force on proton 1 will be zero, when
    {tex}\frac{e r^{2} 2^{*}}{4 \varepsilon_{0} \pi R^{4}}=\frac{e^{2}}{16 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow r^{4}=\frac{R^{4}}{8}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow r=\frac{R}{(8)^{1 / 4}}{/tex}
    Therefore, the distance of both the protons from the centre must be {tex}r=\frac{R}{(8)^{1 / 4}}{/tex}.

question_text: Two fixed, identical conducting plates ( {tex}\alpha{/tex} & {tex}\beta{/tex} ), each of surface area S are charged to – Q and q , respectively, where Q > q > 0. A third identical plate ( {tex}\gamma{/tex} ), free to move is located on the other side of the plate with charge q at a distance d (Fig). The third plate is released and collides with the plate {tex}\beta{/tex} . Assume the collision is elastic and the time of collision is sufficient to redistribute charge amongst {tex}\beta{/tex} & {tex}\gamma{/tex} Find the electric field acting on the plate {tex}\gamma{/tex} before collision. Find the charges on {tex}\beta{/tex} and {tex}\gamma{/tex} after the collision. Find the velocity of the plate {tex}\gamma{/tex} after the collision and at a distance d from the plate {tex}\beta{/tex} .
question_html:
Two fixed, identical conducting plates ({tex}\alpha{/tex} & {tex}\beta{/tex}), each of surface area S are charged to – Q and q, respectively, where Q > q > 0. A third identical plate ({tex}\gamma{/tex}), free to move is located on the other side of the plate with charge q at a distance d (Fig). The third plate is released and collides with the plate {tex}\beta{/tex}. Assume the collision is elastic and the time of collision is sufficient to redistribute charge amongst {tex}\beta{/tex} & {tex}\gamma{/tex}

  1. Find the electric field acting on the plate {tex}\gamma{/tex} before collision.
  2. Find the charges on {tex}\beta{/tex} and {tex}\gamma{/tex} after the collision.
  3. Find the velocity of the plate {tex}\gamma{/tex} after the collision and at a distance d from the plate {tex}\beta{/tex}.
solution_text: Net electric field at plate {tex}\gamma{/tex} before collision is vector sum of electric field at plate {tex}\gamma{/tex} due to plate {tex}\alpha{/tex} and {tex}\beta{/tex} . Considering the right direction to be positive The electric field at {tex}\gamma{/tex} due to the plate {tex}\alpha{/tex} is {tex}-\frac{Q}{S\left(2 \epsilon_{o}\right)}{/tex} [i.e., towards left] The electric field at {tex}\gamma{/tex} due to the plate {tex}\beta{/tex} is {tex}\frac{Q}{S\left(2 \epsilon_{o}\right)}{/tex} [i.e., towards right] The net electric field = {tex}\frac{q-Q}{S\left(2 \in_{o}\right)}{/tex} [Here, Q>q, so the direction will be towards left During the collision plates {tex}\beta{/tex} and {tex}\gamma{/tex} are together so they must be at one potential. Charge on {tex}\beta{/tex} is q 1 and on {tex}\gamma{/tex} is q 2 . Consider a point O, in between the plates. The electric field here must be zero. Electric field at O due to {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-Q}{S\left(2 \varepsilon_{0}\right)}{/tex} , to the left Electric field at O due to {tex}\beta=\frac{q_{1}}{S\left(2 \varepsilon_{0}\right)},{/tex} to the right Electric field at O due {tex}\gamma=\frac{q_{2}}{S\left(2 \varepsilon_{0}\right)}{/tex} to the left As the electric field at O is zero, therefore {tex}\frac{Q+q}{S\left(2 \varepsilon_{0}\right)}=\frac{q_{1}}{S\left(2 \varepsilon_{0}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Q + q 2 - q 2 , (i) There is no loss of charge on collision, therefore Q + q = q 1 + q 2 (ii) On solving (i) and (ii), we get q 1 = (Q + {tex}\frac q2{/tex} ) = charge on plates {tex}\beta{/tex} q 2 = ( {tex}\frac q2{/tex} ) = charge on plate {tex}\gamma{/tex} Let the velocity be v at the distance d after the collision. If m is the mass of the plate {tex}\gamma{/tex} , then the gain in K.E. over the round trip must be equal to the work done by the electric field. After the collision, the electric field at {tex}\gamma{/tex} is {tex}E_{2}=\frac{-Q}{2 \varepsilon_{0} S}+\frac{(Q+q / 2)}{2 \varepsilon_{0} S}=\frac{q / 2}{2 \varepsilon_{0} S}{/tex} {tex}F_{2}=E_{2} q / 2=\frac{(q / 2)^{2}}{2 \varepsilon_{0} S}{/tex} Total work done = {tex}\left(F_{1}+F_{2}\right) d=\left[\frac{(Q-q) Q}{2 \varepsilon_{0} S}+\frac{(q / 2)^{2}}{2 \varepsilon_{0} S}\right] d=\frac{(Q-q / 2)^{2} d}{2 \varepsilon_{0} S}{/tex} Using work energy theorem, we have {tex}\frac{1}{2} m w^{2}=\frac{(Q-q / 2)^{2} d}{2 \varepsilon_{0} S}{/tex} Further solving, we have {tex}v=(Q- \frac q {2})\left(\frac{d}{m \varepsilon_{0} S}\right)^{1 / 2}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Net electric field at plate {tex}\gamma{/tex}  before collision is vector sum of electric field at plate {tex}\gamma{/tex} due to plate {tex}\alpha{/tex}and {tex}\beta{/tex}. Considering the right direction to be positive
    The electric field at {tex}\gamma{/tex} due to the plate {tex}\alpha{/tex} is {tex}-\frac{Q}{S\left(2 \epsilon_{o}\right)}{/tex} [i.e., towards left]
    The electric field at {tex}\gamma{/tex} due to the plate {tex}\beta{/tex} is {tex}\frac{Q}{S\left(2 \epsilon_{o}\right)}{/tex} [i.e., towards right]
    The net electric field = {tex}\frac{q-Q}{S\left(2 \in_{o}\right)}{/tex}[Here, Q>q, so the direction will be towards left
  2. During the collision plates {tex}\beta{/tex} and {tex}\gamma{/tex} are together so they must be at one potential. Charge on {tex}\beta{/tex} is q1 and on {tex}\gamma{/tex} is q2 . Consider a point O, in between the plates. The electric field here must be zero.
    Electric field at O due to {tex}\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-Q}{S\left(2 \varepsilon_{0}\right)}{/tex}, to the left
    Electric field at O due to {tex}\beta=\frac{q_{1}}{S\left(2 \varepsilon_{0}\right)},{/tex} to the right
    Electric field at O due {tex}\gamma=\frac{q_{2}}{S\left(2 \varepsilon_{0}\right)}{/tex} to the left
    As the electric field at O is zero, therefore
    {tex}\frac{Q+q}{S\left(2 \varepsilon_{0}\right)}=\frac{q_{1}}{S\left(2 \varepsilon_{0}\right)}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Q + q2 - q2, (i)
    There is no loss of charge on collision, therefore
    Q + q = q1 + q2 (ii)
    On solving (i) and (ii), we get
    q1 = (Q + {tex}\frac q2{/tex}) = charge on plates {tex}\beta{/tex}
    q2 = ({tex}\frac q2{/tex}) = charge on plate {tex}\gamma{/tex}
  3. Let the velocity be v at the distance d after the collision. If m is the mass of the plate {tex}\gamma{/tex}, then the gain in K.E. over the round trip must be equal to the work done by the electric field. After the collision, the electric field at {tex}\gamma{/tex} is
    {tex}E_{2}=\frac{-Q}{2 \varepsilon_{0} S}+\frac{(Q+q / 2)}{2 \varepsilon_{0} S}=\frac{q / 2}{2 \varepsilon_{0} S}{/tex}
    {tex}F_{2}=E_{2} q / 2=\frac{(q / 2)^{2}}{2 \varepsilon_{0} S}{/tex}
    Total work done ={tex}\left(F_{1}+F_{2}\right) d=\left[\frac{(Q-q) Q}{2 \varepsilon_{0} S}+\frac{(q / 2)^{2}}{2 \varepsilon_{0} S}\right] d=\frac{(Q-q / 2)^{2} d}{2 \varepsilon_{0} S}{/tex}
    Using work energy theorem, we have
    {tex}\frac{1}{2} m w^{2}=\frac{(Q-q / 2)^{2} d}{2 \varepsilon_{0} S}{/tex}
    Further solving, we have
    {tex}v=(Q- \frac q {2})\left(\frac{d}{m \varepsilon_{0} S}\right)^{1 / 2}{/tex}

question_text: There is another useful system of units, besides the SI/mks. A system called the cgs (centimeter-gram-second) system. In this system Coloumb’s law is given by {tex}\mathbf{F}=\frac{\mathrm{Q} \mathrm{q}}{r^{2}} \boldsymbol{\boldsymbol { r }}{/tex} where the distance r is measured in cm (=10 -2 m), F in dynes (=10 -5 N) and the charges in electrostatic units (es units), where 1es unit of charge = {tex}\frac{1}{[3]} \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{C}{/tex} The number [3] actually arises from the speed of light in a vacuum which is now taken to be exactly given by c = 2.99792458 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s. The approximate value of c then is c = [3] {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s. Show that the coloumb law in cgs units yields 1 esu of charge = 1 (dyne) 1/2 cm Obtain the dimensions of units of charge in terms of mass M, length L, and time T. Show that it is given in terms of fractional powers of M and L. Write 1 esu of charge = x C, where x is a dimensionless number. Show that this gives {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}=\frac{10^{-9}}{x^{2}} \frac{\mathrm{N} \cdot \mathrm{m}^{2}}{\mathrm{C}^{2}}{/tex} With {tex}x=\frac{1}{[3]} \times 10^{-9},{/tex} we have {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}=[3]^{2} \times 10^{9} \frac{\mathrm{Nm}^{2}}{\mathrm{C}^{2}}{/tex} or, {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}={/tex} (2.99792458) 2 {tex}\times 10^{9} \frac{\mathrm{Nm}^{2}}{\mathrm{C}^{2}}{/tex} (exactly).
question_html:
There is another useful system of units, besides the SI/mks. A system called the cgs (centimeter-gram-second) system. In this system Coloumb’s law is given by {tex}\mathbf{F}=\frac{\mathrm{Q} \mathrm{q}}{r^{2}} \boldsymbol{\boldsymbol { r }}{/tex} where the distance r is measured in cm (=10-2 m), F in dynes (=10-5 N) and the charges in electrostatic units (es units), where 1es unit of charge = {tex}\frac{1}{[3]} \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{C}{/tex}
The number [3] actually arises from the speed of light in a vacuum which is now taken to be exactly given by c = 2.99792458 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m/s. The approximate value of c then is c = [3] {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m/s.

  1. Show that the coloumb law in cgs units yields
    1 esu of charge = 1 (dyne)1/2 cm
    Obtain the dimensions of units of charge in terms of mass M, length L, and time T. Show that it is given in terms of fractional powers of M and L.
  2. Write 1 esu of charge = x C, where x is a dimensionless number. Show that this gives
    {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}=\frac{10^{-9}}{x^{2}} \frac{\mathrm{N} \cdot \mathrm{m}^{2}}{\mathrm{C}^{2}}{/tex}
    With {tex}x=\frac{1}{[3]} \times 10^{-9},{/tex} we have
    {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}=[3]^{2} \times 10^{9} \frac{\mathrm{Nm}^{2}}{\mathrm{C}^{2}}{/tex}
    or, {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{0}}={/tex} (2.99792458)2{tex}\times 10^{9} \frac{\mathrm{Nm}^{2}}{\mathrm{C}^{2}}{/tex} (exactly).
solution_text: {tex}F=\frac{O q}{r^{2}}=1 d y n e=\frac{[1 \text { esu of charge }]^{2}}{[1 \mathrm{cm}]^{2}}{/tex} 1 esu of charge =1(dyne) 1/2 cm Hence, [1 esu of charge] = [F] 1/2 L = [MLT -2 ] 1/2 L = M 1/2 L 3/2 T -1 [1 esu of charge] = M 1/2 L 3/2 T -1 Thus charge in cgs unit is expressed as fractional powers ( {tex}\frac 12{/tex} ) of M and ( {tex}\frac 32{/tex} ) of L Consider the coloumb force on two charges, each of magnitude 1 esu of charge separated by a distance of 1 cm: The force is then 1 dyne = 10 -5 N This situation is equivalent to two charges of magnitude xC separated by 10 -2 m This gives: {tex}F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{x^{2}}{10^{-4}}{/tex} which should be 1 dyne = 10 -5 N. Thus {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{o}} \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{10^{-4}}=10^{-5} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{10^{-9}}{x^{2}} \frac{\mathrm{Nm}^{2}}{\mathrm{C}^{2}}{/tex} With {tex}x=\frac{1}{[3] \times 10^{9}}{/tex} , this yields {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}=10^{-9} \times[3]^{2} \times 10^{18}=[3]^{2} \times 10^{9} \frac{\mathrm{Nm}^{2}}{\mathrm{C}^{2}}{/tex} With [3] {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2.99792458, we have {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}=8.98755 \ldots \times 10^{9} \frac{\mathrm{Nm}^{2}}{\mathrm{C}^{2}}{/tex} exactly.
solution_html:
1. {tex}F=\frac{O q}{r^{2}}=1 d y n e=\frac{[1 \text { esu of charge }]^{2}}{[1 \mathrm{cm}]^{2}}{/tex}
    1 esu of charge =1(dyne)1/2cm
    Hence, [1 esu of charge] = [F]1/2 L = [MLT-2]1/2 L = M1/2 L3/2 T-1
    [1 esu of charge] = M1/2 L3/2 T-1
    Thus charge in cgs unit is expressed as fractional powers ({tex}\frac 12{/tex}) of M and ({tex}\frac 32{/tex}) of L
  2. Consider the coloumb force on two charges, each of magnitude 1 esu of charge separated by a distance of 1 cm:
    The force is then 1 dyne = 10-5 N
    This situation is equivalent to two charges of magnitude xC separated by 10-2 m
    This gives:
    {tex}F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \frac{x^{2}}{10^{-4}}{/tex}
    which should be 1 dyne = 10-5 N. Thus
    {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{o}} \cdot \frac{x^{2}}{10^{-4}}=10^{-5} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{10^{-9}}{x^{2}} \frac{\mathrm{Nm}^{2}}{\mathrm{C}^{2}}{/tex}
    With  {tex}x=\frac{1}{[3] \times 10^{9}}{/tex}, this yields
    {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}=10^{-9} \times[3]^{2} \times 10^{18}=[3]^{2} \times 10^{9} \frac{\mathrm{Nm}^{2}}{\mathrm{C}^{2}}{/tex}
    With [3] {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2.99792458, we have
    {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}}=8.98755 \ldots \times 10^{9} \frac{\mathrm{Nm}^{2}}{\mathrm{C}^{2}}{/tex} exactly.

question_text: Two charges –q each are fixed separated by distance 2d. A third charge q of mass m placed at the mid-point is displaced slightly by x (x<<d) perpendicular to the line joining the two fixed charged as shown in Fig. {tex}T=\left[\frac{8 \pi^{3} \varepsilon_{0} m d^{3}}{q^{2}}\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex}
question_html:
Two charges –q each are fixed separated by distance 2d. A third charge q of mass m placed at the mid-point is displaced slightly by x (x<<d) perpendicular to the line joining the two fixed charged as shown in Fig.

{tex}T=\left[\frac{8 \pi^{3} \varepsilon_{0} m d^{3}}{q^{2}}\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex}
solution_text: In fig. two charges are -q are held at a distance of 2d. Horizontal component of the forces cancel each other and the vertical component gives the net amount of force. So , Net force F on q towards the centre O {tex}F=2 \frac{q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{o} r^{2}} \cos \theta=-\frac{2 q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}} \cdot \frac{x}{r}{/tex} {tex}F=-\frac{2 q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{o}} \frac{x}{\left(d^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex} {tex}\approx-\frac{2 q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{o} d^{3}} x=-k ; \text { for } x<<d{/tex} Thus, the force on the third charge q is proportional to the displacement and is towards the centre of the two other charges. Therefore, the motion of the third charge is harmonic with frequency {tex}\omega=\sqrt{\frac{\bar{K}}{m}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow T=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{K}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m .4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d^{3}}{2 q^{2}}}=\left[\frac{8 \pi^{3} \varepsilon_{0} m d^{3}}{q^{2}}\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex}
solution_html:
In fig. two charges are -q are held at a distance of 2d. Horizontal component of the forces cancel each other and the vertical component gives the net amount of force. So , Net force F on q towards the centre O
{tex}F=2 \frac{q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{o} r^{2}} \cos \theta=-\frac{2 q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} r^{2}} \cdot \frac{x}{r}{/tex}
{tex}F=-\frac{2 q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{o}} \frac{x}{\left(d^{2}+x^{2}\right)^{3 / 2}}{/tex}
{tex}\approx-\frac{2 q^{2}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{o} d^{3}} x=-k ; \text { for } x<<d{/tex}
Thus, the force on the third charge q is proportional to the displacement and is towards the centre of the two other charges.
Therefore, the motion of the third charge is harmonic with frequency
{tex}\omega=\sqrt{\frac{\bar{K}}{m}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow T=\frac{2 \pi}{\omega}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{K}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m .4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} d^{3}}{2 q^{2}}}=\left[\frac{8 \pi^{3} \varepsilon_{0} m d^{3}}{q^{2}}\right]^{1 / 2}{/tex}

question_text: .Total charge –Q is uniformly spread along the length of a ring of radius R. A small test charge +q of mass m is kept at the centre of the ring and is given a gentle push along the axis of the ring. Show that the particle executes a simple harmonic oscillation. Obtain its time period.
question_html:
.Total charge –Q is uniformly spread along the length of a ring of radius R. A small test charge +q of mass m is kept at the centre of the ring and is given a gentle push along the axis of the ring.

  1. Show that the particle executes a simple harmonic oscillation.
  2. Obtain its time period.
solution_text: Let us consider q is displaced slightly by z along the axis of the ring. The motion is simple harmonic if q must be proportional to Z and is directed towards O. A slight push on q along the axis of the ring gives rise to the situation shown in Fig (b). solution_html:
Let us consider q is displaced slightly by z along the axis of the ring. The motion is simple harmonic if q must be proportional to Z and is directed towards O.

  1. A slight push on q along the axis of the ring gives rise to the situation shown in Fig (b). A and B are two points on the ring at the end of a diameter
    Force on q due to line elements {tex}\frac{-Q }{ 2 \pi R}{/tex} at A and B is
    {tex}F_{A+B}=2 \cdot \frac{-Q}{2 \pi R} \cdot q \cdot \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} \cdot \frac{1}{r^{2}} \cos \theta{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{-O q}{\pi R \cdot 4 \pi \varepsilon_{o}} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(z^{2}+R^{2}\right)} \cdot \frac{z}{\left(z^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}}{/tex}
    Total force due to ring on q = (FA+B)({tex}\pi{/tex} R)
    {tex}=\frac{-Q q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{o}} \cdot \frac{1}{\left(z^{2}+R^{2}\right)} \cdot \frac{z}{\left(z^{2}+R^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{-Q q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{0}} f o r z<<R{/tex}
    Thus, the force is proportional to the negative of displacement. The motion under such forces is harmonic.
  2. From results in option (a),
    {tex}m \frac{d^{2} z}{d t^{2}}=-\frac{Q q z}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{o} R^{3}} \text { or } \frac{d^{2} z}{d t^{2}}=-\frac{Q q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{o} m R^{3}} z{/tex}
    That is, {tex}\omega^{2}=\frac{Q q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_{o} m R^{3}}{/tex} 
    Therefore, {tex}T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{4 \pi \varepsilon_{o} m R}{Q q}}{/tex}
